message.content: <local-command-caveat>Caveat: The messages below were generated by the user while running local commands. DO NOT respond to these messages or otherwise consider them in your response unless the user explicitly asks you to.</local-command-caveat>

Dans l'exemple sur les Terraform test pour le dernier live, dérouille-toi pour corriger la CI, je crois qu'il y a des problèmes de lint quelque part qui ne passent pas, voilà, donc fais le nécessaire sur le sujet en regardant les erreurs côté GitHub.

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- entire-search: Search Entire checkpoint history and transcripts with `entire search --json`. Use proactively when the user asks about previous work, commits, sessions, prompts, or historical context in this repository. (Tools: Bash)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

addedBlocks:
## context7
Use this server to fetch current documentation whenever the user asks about a library, framework, SDK, API, CLI tool, or cloud service -- even well-known ones like React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, Tailwind, Django, or Spring Boot. This includes API syntax, configuration, version migration, library-specific debugging, setup instructions, and CLI tool usage. Use even when you think you know the answer -- your training data may not reflect recent changes. Prefer this over web search for library docs.

Do not use for: refactoring, writing scripts from scratch, debugging business logic, code review, or general programming concepts.

attachment.content:
- handoff: End-of-session handoff. Reconcile the project's tracking file (mark done resolved, promote every leftover to a live item with value+trigger, log decisions/debt/lessons), verify green via the project's oracle command, confirm nothing lives only in chat or a disposable note, persist a disposable resume prompt (with a cold-start reload list of the links that mattered this session) into the tracking file, checkpoint-commit, then compact the note. Project-agnostic. Use when wrapping up, pausing, "prep handoff", "close the session", or "generate a resume prompt".
- linkedin-review: Multi-agent parallel review of a LinkedIn profile against benchmarks, via Playwright MCP browser navigation. Spawns N specialized personas (decideur-client, commercial-esn, personal-branding, concurrent-freelance, copywriter, ats-keywords, seo-linkedin) in parallel, each producing an independent critique. Returns a synthesized report with convergences, conflicts, prioritized actions. Use when the user asks to audit/review their LinkedIn profile.
- review-plan: Multi-agent parallel review of a plan, design doc, or architecture proposal. Spawns N expert personas (product, security, performance, seo, devex, cloud-architecture, finops, compliance, accessibility) in parallel, each producing an independent critique. Returns a synthesized report with convergences, conflicts, and decisions to make. Use when the user asks to review a plan, audit a design, get multiple perspectives on a doc, or run a "multi-agent review".
- rtk: RTK (Rust Token Killer) proxy meta-commands. Use when the user asks for RTK token-savings analytics, command history, missed-opportunity discovery, or wants to bypass the auto-rewrite hook (rtk proxy). The PreToolUse hook auto-rewrites Bash commands transparently - do not invoke this skill for normal commands.
- add-contact: Create one CRM Contact via add-contact.py (deterministic, schema-validated). Use when adding a person with optional Org affiliations. For multi-entity free text, use add-lead.
- add-editorial-idea: Capture a new editorial idea (LinkedIn post, article, talk seed) as an anchor in `02-Areas/Éditorial/Ideas/`. Runs dedup check, applies frontmatter schema, enforces BGA brand conformity, updates MoC. Auto-detects express vs standard mode. Use when the user wants to capture, add, save, or register an editorial idea, post idea, article idea, or content seed.
- add-editorial-piece: Transform an editorial anchor (`Ideas/<file>.md`) into a piece for a given format (linkedin, article, live, podcast, talk). Resolves the anchor, fills frontmatter from template SSoT, drafts the body, runs brand conformity, ticks the matching line in the anchor's Déclinaisons checklist. Use when the user wants to draft a LinkedIn post, article, talk, live, or podcast piece from an existing idea.
- add-editorial-slides: Generate a branded HTML slide deck for a piece from the BGA slides skeleton. Composes slides from the piece narrative (Why/How/What, key points, tables) using slide-type primitives (title/bullets/cards/table/flow), renders each slide for a legibility gate, runs brand-review, wires the deck back into the piece. Interactive HTML opened in the browser, no delivery PNG. Use when the user wants intro slides, a live deck, or a talk/workshop deck for an editorial piece.
- add-editorial-visual: Generate a branded editorial visual (square post visual or LinkedIn event banner) for a piece from the BGA HTML skeleton. Reads the piece's `## Visuels` brief (format + disposition + setup/Why + contenu/How + synthèse/What), composes with CSS auto-layout (never hand-placed coordinates), renders HTML->PNG via Playwright, gates on visual legibility + brand-review, wires the asset back into the piece. Use when the user wants a schema, diagram, before/after, lifecycle visual, or event banner for a LinkedIn post, article, or live.
- add-formation: Bootstrap a new BGA formation by aggregating N editorial anchors (`Ideas/*.md`) into a curriculum. Creates the formation folder, generates the index from template, scaffolds one module file per anchor, backlinks each anchor via its Déclinaisons checklist, updates the Formations MoC. Supports direct mode (--ideas arg) or interactive mode (anchor picker grouped by pillar). Use when the user wants to create, scaffold, build, or initialize a training, workshop, masterclass, or curriculum.
- add-interaction: Create one CRM Interaction (dated exchange report) via add-interaction.py (deterministic, schema-validated). Use to log a call/meeting/email/event tied to an Opp or Org.
- add-lead: Add a new prospect (Organisation + optional Opportunity + optional Contact) to the CRM. Accepts free text and/or LinkedIn URLs. Use when the user mentions a new company or contact to add.
- add-opportunity: Create one CRM Opportunity via add-opp.py (deterministic, schema-validated). Use when adding a new channel/deal on an existing or new target Org. One Opp = one channel.
- add-organisation: Create one CRM Organisation via add-org.py (deterministic, schema-validated). Use when adding a single company (no opportunity/contact). For multi-entity free text + LinkedIn, use add-lead.
- archive-opportunity: Move a closed CRM Opportunity (perdu/gagne) to Opportunities/_archive/ via archive-opp.py (git mv, FK-safe). Use to declutter active pipeline after a deal closes.
- archive-project: Guided PARA archival of a finished project. Extracts forward-looking artefacts BEFORE the move - active backlog to Projects, living doc/SSoT to an existing or newly-created Area, structural decisions to ADRs, pure reference to Resources - sets the frozen marker, git mv to 04-Archives, propagates orphan wikilinks. Judgment-heavy (classify + route); writes = git mv + targeted edits. Use when a project is done and must be archived.
- brand-review: Audit a BGA editorial artifact (text and/or visual) against brand SSoT. Verifies tone-of-voice principles, format specs, color tokens, WCAG ratios, text/visual coherence. Outputs a status table + one prioritized recommendation.
- parse-td-results: Parse a Tutorial Dojo HTML exam result, extract incorrect questions, and append summaries to the AWS SAP Mistakes note.
- synthesize-interaction: Synthesize a freelance prospection Interaction draft - restructure the raw note and propagate to the linked Opportunity/Organisation. Thick orchestrator - LLM synthesis only, writes delegated to deterministic scripts.
- update-contact: Update an existing CRM Contact via update-contact.py (deterministic, schema-validated, idempotent). Set personal fields, append Org affiliations or notes.
- update-lead: Quick update of an existing Organisation or Opportunity from free text (informal conversation, phone call, ad-hoc info). Auto-detects the target. Use when the user reports info about an existing prospect.
- update-opportunity: Update an existing CRM Opportunity (non-status fields) via update-opp.py (deterministic, schema-validated, idempotent). For status/lost/won transitions use transition-opp.
- update-organisation: Update an existing CRM Organisation via update-org.py (deterministic, schema-validated, idempotent). Set durable company fields or append to body regions.
- weekly-actions: Display prospection actions for the current calendar week (Mon-Sun), including overdue items. Source - Opportunities. Use when the user asks for actions, planning, or prospection to-do.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

aiTitle: Corriger les erreurs de lint dans la CI Terraform

input.command:
echo "===== conventional commits =====" && gh run view --job 83194927079 --log-failed 2>&1 | grep -iE "subject|type|error|fail|lower|empty|enum|leading|may not|must" | head -25
echo ""
echo "===== deferred-actions/02-after =====" && gh run view --job 83194946160 --log-failed 2>&1 | grep -iE "issue|error|warning|Process completed|Failed|Error:" | head -25

content.subject: Construire l'exemple Terraform (module S3 + tests)
content.description: FAIT : examples/terraform-module-testing/ - module S3 (validations simples+croisées, KMS conditionnel), 4 couches de test 14/14 vertes (validations expect_failures, plan mock creds-free, deploy réel AWS eu-west-1, parallel+state_key), tag+sweeper. Commit f67c7e4, branche feat/terraform-module-testing poussée, PR #33. Finding : SCP sandbox region-lock S3 -> eu-west-1.

content.subject: Créer l'anchor éditorial Terraform testing
content.description: add-editorial-idea : nouvel anchor type idee-editoriale, pillar iac, angle = prouver le comportement d'un module et le faire échouer proprement, sans déployer en prod. Cross-link vers l'anchor deferred-actions (continuité KMS→S3). Déclinaisons : Live/Podcast (cette pièce) + LinkedIn (annonce, puis retex post-live).

content.subject: Rédiger la pièce Live (conducteur + notes prod)
content.description: FAIT : pièce live complète - accroche, fil rouge 13 features, conducteur DÉTAILLÉ sur le vrai code (commandes make réelles, rouge→vert par édition live d'une validation, eu-west-1/SCP, parallel, sweeper), tips, pre-flight réel (make init/test-fast/test, merge PR #33), briefs visuels, notes prod. Lint clean. Statut draft (passe published après le live).

content.subject: Rédiger l'annonce LinkedIn (texte event)
content.description: FAIT (côté user) : event LinkedIn Live programmé via Restream avec titre `❯ live tech_ · Terraform test` + description (fournie, 700 car., rangée dans la pièce Notes prod) + bannière. Lien code + stream en 1er commentaire. Promotion gérée par Baptiste.

content.subject: Générer event-banner + vignette rappel
content.description: FAIT : (1) event-banner 1600x900 (marque ❯ live tech_, sous-titre Why→How→What) - PRÊT, câblé. (2) vignette rappel square 1200x1200 (assets/square-terraform-test-reminder, marque série + Aujourd'hui 13h + 3 gestes valider→casser→déployer + LinkedIn/Twitch) - legibility PASS, tokens whitelist, câblée. Les deux brand-review PRÊT.

content.subject: Générer le deck slides intro (optionnel)
content.description: FAIT : assets/slides-terraform-test.html - deck intro 6 slides (titre avec marque ❯ live tech_, Why module=décisions, pyramide→diamant, comparatif outils table, reco layering flow, transition démo). Légibilité PASS (4 slides inspectées), tokens 8/8 whitelist, zéro em-dash, brand-review inline PRÊT. Câblé dans la pièce Notes prod.

content.subject: Hygiène + intégration process éditorial
content.description: FAIT : lint pièce clean, Déclinaisons anchor tickée, Dashboard à jour, 2 commits vault ciblés (bcc3c4b0 = anchor+pièce+bannière+MoC+Dashboard ; 097fc82c = slides+vignette+conducteur détaillé). settings.local.json non tracké (reste local). Message de rappel J fourni.

content.subject: Analyse comparative terraform test vs alternatives
content.description: FAIT (via slides) : comparatif rendu dans la slide 4 du deck - terraform test (HCL) vs Terratest (Go, e2e/polling) vs terraform-compliance (BDD) + couche statique (tflint/trivy/OPA, créds-less), axes langage/couverture/quand + note pourquoi-maintenant. Recherche R2 (kitchen-terraform archivé, Terragrunt non-test, tfsec→Trivy). Currency KICS/Regula/Snyk = non bloquant, hors slide.
content.activeForm: Analyse comparative des outils de test

content.subject: Gouvernance identité de série live tech_ (marque + titre, Option 3)
content.description: Passe dédiée post-live. Gouvernance de l'identité de série (visuelle ET textuelle), Option 3 décidée 2026-06-24. (A) MARQUE visuelle ❯ live tech_ : canoniser en §5.5 (markup HTML+CSS unique, vocab .series-mark/.sm-prompt/.sm-hl/.sm-cursor), élément FIXE dans event-banner/carrousel/stream/slides (unifier classes divergentes carrousel .series/.stk/.cur vs stream .label/.hl/.cursor ; ajouter event-banner + auditer slides), skills slots-only. (B) CONVENTION DE TITRE des lives : titre = `❯ live tech_ · <sujet>`, documentée en §5.5 (sibling textuel de la marque), add-editorial-piece (format live) préfixe auto, note Éditorial/CLAUDE.md + commentaire template piece. (C) ADR commun (décision structurante) + check lint (squelettes portent le bloc canonique ; titres format:live conformes) + bump design-system version + journal README brand. Drift constaté 2026-06-24 : event-banner sans la marque, classes divergentes, titres ad-hoc.
content.activeForm: Canonicalisation de la marque de série

content.subject: Publier la VOD + repartage page BGA (live Terraform test)
content.description: Boucle Suivre, J après-midi / J+1 : publier la VOD du live (YouTube/LinkedIn), repartage par la page BGA. Reporter l'URL VOD dans la pièce live + Dashboard.

content.subject: Carrousel retex - live Terraform test
content.description: Boucle Suivre, J+ : carrousel LinkedIn de repurpose (retex du live), pattern design-system §5.8 (comme live #1 deferred actions). Arc Why→How→What, livré PDF, lié à l'anchor terraform test (Déclinaisons LinkedIn). Hand-craft depuis skeletons/carousel.html.

content.subject: Capturer les leads du live dans le CRM (canal live)
content.description: Boucle Suivre, J+ : capter les leads issus du live (commentaires, DM, inscrits event, échanges Q&A) dans le CRM via add-lead, canal sourcing 'live'. Lier à la pièce (leads[]).

aiTitle: Corriger les erreurs de lint dans la CI Terraform

content.text: Le `enable_encryption` est référencé par les tests (`expect_failures`, plan assertions) mais jamais lu dans le code du module - le gating se fait sur l'ARN seul. Vérifions les conventions de version locales avant de corriger.

input.prompt: What does this page say about best practices for provider version constraints? Specifically, what is recommended for reusable/child modules versus root modules regarding the use of >= versus ~> (pessimistic) constraints? Quote the exact best-practice guidance verbatim.

Explain the version constraint operators, especially ~> (pessimistic / tilde-equal). What does the page recommend about module version constraint best practices, and the difference between using ~> and >= ? Quote any best-practices or recommended-constraint-syntax guidance verbatim, including any guidance about required_version for Terraform core.

content.content:
# Best Practices for Provider Version Constraints

According to the documentation, there are distinct recommendations based on module type:

**For Root Modules:**
The guidance states to "specify the _maximum_ provider version it is intended to work with, to avoid accidental upgrades to incompatible new versions." The `~>` operator is recommended as "a convenient shorthand for allowing the rightmost component of a version to increment."

**For Reusable/Child Modules:**
The documentation explicitly advises: "Do not use `~>` (or other maximum-version constraints) for modules you intend to reuse across many configurations, even if you know the module isn't compatible with certain newer versions."

Instead, the guidance states to "Specify a minimum version, document any known incompatibilities, and let the root module manage the maximum version."

In summary: use `~>` constraints for root modules only, and use `>=` for minimum versions in reusable modules to avoid forcing downstream users into simultaneous multi-module upgrades.

According to the documentation, there are distinct recommendations based on module type:

**For Root Modules:**
The guidance states to "specify the _maximum_ provider version it is intended to work with, to avoid accidental upgrades to incompatible new versions." The `~>` operator is recommended as "a convenient shorthand for allowing the rightmost component of a version to increment."

**For Reusable/Child Modules:**
The documentation explicitly advises: "Do not use `~>` (or other maximum-version constraints) for modules you intend to reuse across many configurations, even if you know the module isn't compatible with certain newer versions."

Instead, the guidance states to "Specify a minimum version, document any known incompatibilities, and let the root module manage the maximum version."

In summary: use `~>` constraints for root modules only, and use `>=` for minimum versions in reusable modules to avoid forcing downstream users into simultaneous multi-module upgrades.

content.content:
# Terraform Version Constraint Operators

## The Operators

Terraform supports these constraint operators:

- **`=` or no operator**: Exact version only
- **`!=`**: Excludes a specific version
- **`>`, `>=`, `<`, `<=`**: Standard comparisons
- **`~>` (pessimistic)**: Allows only the rightmost version component to increment

## The Tilde-Equal Operator (~>)

The `~>` operator is particularly useful for constraining updates. According to the documentation:

> "~> 1.0.4: Allows Terraform to install 1.0.5 and 1.0.10 but not 1.1.0. ~> 1.1: Allows Terraform to install 1.2 and 1.10 but not 2.0."

## Best Practices: Module Versions vs. Terraform Core

The page distinguishes between two scenarios:

**For third-party modules**, the recommendation is to:
> "Require specific versions to ensure that updates only happen when convenient to you when your infrastructure depends on third-party modules."

**For root modules using providers**, the guidance states:
> "Root modules should use a `~>` constraint to set both a lower and upper bound on versions for each provider they depend on."

However, **reusable modules** should follow a different approach:
> "Reusable modules should constrain only their minimum allowed versions of Terraform and providers, such as `>= 0.12.0`."

## The Key Difference

Using `>=` alone allows unlimited upgrades (flexibility), while `~>` establishes both a floor and ceiling, preventing major version surprises.

- **`!=`**: Excludes a specific version
- **`>`, `>=`, `<`, `<=`**: Standard comparisons
- **`~>` (pessimistic)**: Allows only the rightmost version component to increment

## The Tilde-Equal Operator (~>)

The `~>` operator is particularly useful for constraining updates. According to the documentation:

> "~> 1.0.4: Allows Terraform to install 1.0.5 and 1.0.10 but not 1.1.0. ~> 1.1: Allows Terraform to install 1.2 and 1.10 but not 2.0."

## Best Practices: Module Versions vs. Terraform Core

The page distinguishes between two scenarios:

**For third-party modules**, the recommendation is to:
> "Require specific versions to ensure that updates only happen when convenient to you when your infrastructure depends on third-party modules."

**For root modules using providers**, the guidance states:
> "Root modules should use a `~>` constraint to set both a lower and upper bound on versions for each provider they depend on."

However, **reusable modules** should follow a different approach:
> "Reusable modules should constrain only their minimum allowed versions of Terraform and providers, such as `>= 0.12.0`."

## The Key Difference

Using `>=` alone allows unlimited upgrades (flexibility), while `~>` establishes both a floor and ceiling, preventing major version surprises.

input.description: Dump exact commit body bytes

content.content:
# Terraform `required_version` Best Practices

Based on the documentation provided, **there is no specific recommendation for using `~>` (pessimistic) versus `>=` operators** for the `required_version` setting.

The page only notes a general collaborative use case:

> "Use Terraform version constraints in a collaborative environment to ensure that everyone is using a specific Terraform version, or using at least a minimum Terraform version that has behavior expected by the configuration."

The documentation explains that `required_version` applies only to the Terraform CLI version (not provider versions) and that all constraints—including those in modules—must be satisfied collectively. However, **it does not differentiate between best practices for root modules versus reusable/shared modules**, nor does it recommend a particular operator pattern.

To find guidance on choosing between `~>` and `>=` for modules, you would need to consult additional resources beyond this reference page.

input.query: version constraint best practices: pessimistic ~> operator for root modules to set lower and upper bound vs >= minimum version for reusable child modules; required_version recommendation

content.text:
context7 confirme (source: developer.hashicorp.com), identique à ma trouvaille fetch mais via le bon canal :
- **Root**: "specify both minimum and maximum versions using `~>`" - exemple `required_version = "~> 1.7.5"`.
- **Reusable/child**: "declare the minimum provider version using `>=`. Avoid using maximum version constraints for reusable modules."

Ta logique est juste. Le `tests/setup` est root-like (appliqué standalone par `terraform test`). Je corrige en `~>`.

lastPrompt: Dans l'exemple sur les Terraform test pour le dernier live, dérouille-toi pour corriger la CI, je crois qu'il y a des problèmes de lint quelque part qui ne passent pas, voilà, donc fais le nécessaire…

aiTitle: Corriger les erreurs de lint dans la CI Terraform

content.text: Trivy gate sur **AWS-0132 (HIGH)**: le bucket S3 par défaut (root avec `enable_encryption=false`, `kms_key_arn=null`) n'a pas de chiffrement CMK. C'est **intentionnel et testé** (`no_encryption_block_without_key` asserte 0 bloc de chiffrement par défaut) - forcer le chiffrement casserait ce test. Le job échouait avant au tflint, donc trivy n'avait jamais tourné. Je regarde comment le repo gère déjà les ignores trivy.

input.command: echo "=== .trivyignore files ===" && find . -name ".trivyignore*" -not -path "*/.git/*" 2>/dev/null && echo "" && echo "=== inline trivy:ignore / AVD / AWS-0 refs ===" && grep -rniE "trivy:ignore|AVD-AWS|AWS-0[0-9]|checkov:skip|tfsec:ignore" --include="*.tf" --include=".trivyignore" --include="*.yml" --include="*.yaml" . 2>/dev/null | grep -v "/.git/" | head -30 && echo "" && echo "=== trivy config in workflow ===" && grep -rniE "trivyignore|trivy.yaml|trivy.yml|misconfig|--ignorefile|severity" .github/ 2>/dev/null | head -20
input.description: Find existing trivy ignore conventions in repo

attachment.stdout: {"hookSpecificOutput":{"hookEventName":"PreToolUse","permissionDecisionReason":"RTK auto-rewrite","updatedInput":{"command":"echo \"=== .trivyignore files ===\" && rtk find . -name \".trivyignore*\" -not -path \"*/.git/*\" 2>/dev/null && echo \"\" && echo \"=== inline trivy:ignore / AVD / AWS-0 refs ===\" && rtk grep -rniE \"trivy:ignore|AVD-AWS|AWS-0[0-9]|checkov:skip|tfsec:ignore\" --include=\"*.tf\" --include=\".trivyignore\" --include=\"*.yml\" --include=\"*.yaml\" . 2>/dev/null | grep -v \"/.git/\" | head -30 && echo \"\" && echo \"=== trivy config in workflow ===\" && rtk grep -rniE \"trivyignore|trivy.yaml|trivy.yml|misconfig|--ignorefile|severity\" .github/ 2>/dev/null | head -20","description":"Find existing trivy ignore conventions in repo"}}}

content.subject: Construire l'exemple Terraform (module S3 + tests)
content.description: FAIT : examples/terraform-module-testing/ - module S3 (validations simples+croisées, KMS conditionnel), 4 couches de test 14/14 vertes (validations expect_failures, plan mock creds-free, deploy réel AWS eu-west-1, parallel+state_key), tag+sweeper. Commit f67c7e4, branche feat/terraform-module-testing poussée, PR #33. Finding : SCP sandbox region-lock S3 -> eu-west-1.

content.subject: Créer l'anchor éditorial Terraform testing
content.description: add-editorial-idea : nouvel anchor type idee-editoriale, pillar iac, angle = prouver le comportement d'un module et le faire échouer proprement, sans déployer en prod. Cross-link vers l'anchor deferred-actions (continuité KMS→S3). Déclinaisons : Live/Podcast (cette pièce) + LinkedIn (annonce, puis retex post-live).

content.subject: Rédiger la pièce Live (conducteur + notes prod)
content.description: FAIT : pièce live complète - accroche, fil rouge 13 features, conducteur DÉTAILLÉ sur le vrai code (commandes make réelles, rouge→vert par édition live d'une validation, eu-west-1/SCP, parallel, sweeper), tips, pre-flight réel (make init/test-fast/test, merge PR #33), briefs visuels, notes prod. Lint clean. Statut draft (passe published après le live).

content.subject: Rédiger l'annonce LinkedIn (texte event)
content.description: FAIT (côté user) : event LinkedIn Live programmé via Restream avec titre `❯ live tech_ · Terraform test` + description (fournie, 700 car., rangée dans la pièce Notes prod) + bannière. Lien code + stream en 1er commentaire. Promotion gérée par Baptiste.

content.subject: Générer event-banner + vignette rappel
content.description: FAIT : (1) event-banner 1600x900 (marque ❯ live tech_, sous-titre Why→How→What) - PRÊT, câblé. (2) vignette rappel square 1200x1200 (assets/square-terraform-test-reminder, marque série + Aujourd'hui 13h + 3 gestes valider→casser→déployer + LinkedIn/Twitch) - legibility PASS, tokens whitelist, câblée. Les deux brand-review PRÊT.

content.subject: Générer le deck slides intro (optionnel)
content.description: FAIT : assets/slides-terraform-test.html - deck intro 6 slides (titre avec marque ❯ live tech_, Why module=décisions, pyramide→diamant, comparatif outils table, reco layering flow, transition démo). Légibilité PASS (4 slides inspectées), tokens 8/8 whitelist, zéro em-dash, brand-review inline PRÊT. Câblé dans la pièce Notes prod.

content.subject: Hygiène + intégration process éditorial
content.description: FAIT : lint pièce clean, Déclinaisons anchor tickée, Dashboard à jour, 2 commits vault ciblés (bcc3c4b0 = anchor+pièce+bannière+MoC+Dashboard ; 097fc82c = slides+vignette+conducteur détaillé). settings.local.json non tracké (reste local). Message de rappel J fourni.

content.subject: Analyse comparative terraform test vs alternatives
content.description: FAIT (via slides) : comparatif rendu dans la slide 4 du deck - terraform test (HCL) vs Terratest (Go, e2e/polling) vs terraform-compliance (BDD) + couche statique (tflint/trivy/OPA, créds-less), axes langage/couverture/quand + note pourquoi-maintenant. Recherche R2 (kitchen-terraform archivé, Terragrunt non-test, tfsec→Trivy). Currency KICS/Regula/Snyk = non bloquant, hors slide.
content.activeForm: Analyse comparative des outils de test

content.subject: Gouvernance identité de série live tech_ (marque + titre, Option 3)
content.description: Passe dédiée post-live. Gouvernance de l'identité de série (visuelle ET textuelle), Option 3 décidée 2026-06-24. (A) MARQUE visuelle ❯ live tech_ : canoniser en §5.5 (markup HTML+CSS unique, vocab .series-mark/.sm-prompt/.sm-hl/.sm-cursor), élément FIXE dans event-banner/carrousel/stream/slides (unifier classes divergentes carrousel .series/.stk/.cur vs stream .label/.hl/.cursor ; ajouter event-banner + auditer slides), skills slots-only. (B) CONVENTION DE TITRE des lives : titre = `❯ live tech_ · <sujet>`, documentée en §5.5 (sibling textuel de la marque), add-editorial-piece (format live) préfixe auto, note Éditorial/CLAUDE.md + commentaire template piece. (C) ADR commun (décision structurante) + check lint (squelettes portent le bloc canonique ; titres format:live conformes) + bump design-system version + journal README brand. Drift constaté 2026-06-24 : event-banner sans la marque, classes divergentes, titres ad-hoc.
content.activeForm: Canonicalisation de la marque de série

content.subject: Publier la VOD + repartage page BGA (live Terraform test)
content.description: Boucle Suivre, J après-midi / J+1 : publier la VOD du live (YouTube/LinkedIn), repartage par la page BGA. Reporter l'URL VOD dans la pièce live + Dashboard.

content.subject: Carrousel retex - live Terraform test
content.description: Boucle Suivre, J+ : carrousel LinkedIn de repurpose (retex du live), pattern design-system §5.8 (comme live #1 deferred actions). Arc Why→How→What, livré PDF, lié à l'anchor terraform test (Déclinaisons LinkedIn). Hand-craft depuis skeletons/carousel.html.

content.subject: Capturer les leads du live dans le CRM (canal live)
content.description: Boucle Suivre, J+ : capter les leads issus du live (commentaires, DM, inscrits event, échanges Q&A) dans le CRM via add-lead, canal sourcing 'live'. Lier à la pièce (leads[]).

content.text: Aucun ignore trivy dans le repo - les autres exemples passent en étant secure-by-default. Voyons comment l'exemple **deferred-actions** (qui crée aussi des buckets S3) passe le gate trivy, c'est la prior art la plus proche.

input.command: echo "=== deferred-actions S3 + encryption ===" && grep -rniE "server_side_encryption|kms|aws_s3_bucket\"|sse_algorithm|trivy:ignore|AVD|AWS-013" examples/terraform-deferred-actions/ --include="*.tf" | head -30 && echo "" && echo "=== trivy workflow step (config/severity/ignore) ===" && grep -rniE "trivy|severity|ignorefile|\.trivy|misconfig|config scan|exit-code" .github/workflows/*.yml | head -30
input.description: Inspect deferred-actions encryption and trivy workflow config

content.content:
=== deferred-actions S3 + encryption ===
examples/terraform-deferred-actions/02-after/outputs.tf:1:output "root_kms_key_arn" {
examples/terraform-deferred-actions/02-after/outputs.tf:2:  description = "ARN of the KMS key created in the root module."
examples/terraform-deferred-actions/02-after/outputs.tf:3:  value       = aws_kms_key.root.arn
examples/terraform-deferred-actions/02-after/outputs.tf:7:  description = "Bucket encrypted with the root-created KMS key."
examples/terraform-deferred-actions/02-after/outputs.tf:10:    kms_key_arn = module.bucket.encryption_kms_key_arn
examples/terraform-deferred-actions/02-after/main.tf:1:# AFTER (Terraform 1.16+): the KMS key ARN is unknown at plan, yet the module's
examples/terraform-deferred-actions/02-after/main.tf:10:# KMS key created in this root module; its ARN is unknown until apply.
examples/terraform-deferred-actions/02-after/main.tf:11:resource "aws_kms_key" "root" {
examples/terraform-deferred-actions/02-after/main.tf:12:  description = "Root-managed KMS key for the deferred-actions S3 demo"
examples/terraform-deferred-actions/02-after/main.tf:23:  kms_key_arn = aws_kms_key.root.arn
examples/terraform-deferred-actions/02-after/modules/s3-bucket-encrypted/outputs.tf:11:output "encryption_kms_key_arn" {
examples/terraform-deferred-actions/02-after/modules/s3-bucket-encrypted/outputs.tf:12:  description = "KMS key ARN used for encryption; null when using S3's default managed key."
examples/terraform-deferred-actions/02-after/modules/s3-bucket-encrypted/outputs.tf:13:  value       = one(data.aws_kms_key.provided[*].arn)
examples/terraform-deferred-actions/02-after/modules/s3-bucket-encrypted/main.tf:2:  # When false, the bucket relies on S3's default SSE-KMS managed key (aws/s3).
examples/terraform-deferred-actions/02-after/modules/s3-bucket-encrypted/main.tf:3:  use_provided_key = var.kms_key_arn != null
examples/terraform-deferred-actions/02-after/modules/s3-bucket-encrypted/main.tf:9:data "aws_kms_key" "provided" {
examples/terraform-deferred-actions/02-after/modules/s3-bucket-encrypted/main.tf:11:  key_id = var.kms_key_arn
examples/terraform-deferred-actions/02-after/modules/s3-bucket-encrypted/main.tf:14:resource "aws_s3_bucket" "this" {
examples/terraform-deferred-actions/02-after/modules/s3-bucket-encrypted/main.tf:36:resource "aws_s3_bucket_server_side_encryption_configuration" "this" {
examples/terraform-deferred-actions/02-after/modules/s3-bucket-encrypted/main.tf:40:    apply_server_side_encryption_by_default {
examples/terraform-deferred-actions/02-after/modules/s3-bucket-encrypted/main.tf:41:      sse_algorithm = "aws:kms"
examples/terraform-deferred-actions/02-after/modules/s3-bucket-encrypted/main.tf:44:      kms_master_key_id = one(data.aws_kms_key.provided[*].arn)
examples/terraform-deferred-actions/02-after/modules/s3-bucket-encrypted/main.tf:46:      # kms_master_key_id = local.use_provided_key ? data.aws_kms_key.provided[0].arn : null
examples/terraform-deferred-actions/02-after/modules/s3-bucket-encrypted/variables.tf:6:variable "kms_key_arn" {
examples/terraform-deferred-actions/02-after/modules/s3-bucket-encrypted/variables.tf:8:    ARN of an existing KMS key to encrypt the bucket with. When null, the bucket
examples/terraform-deferred-actions/02-after/modules/s3-bucket-encrypted/variables.tf:9:    falls back to S3's default SSE-KMS managed key (aws/s3). The value may be
examples/terraform-deferred-actions/01-before/outputs.tf:1:output "root_kms_key_arn" {
examples/terraform-deferred-actions/01-before/outputs.tf:2:  description = "ARN of the KMS key created in the root module."
examples/terraform-deferred-actions/01-before/outputs.tf:3:  value       = aws_kms_key.root.arn
examples/terraform-deferred-actions/01-before/outputs.tf:7:  description = "Bucket encrypted with the root-created KMS key."

=== trivy workflow step (config/severity/ignore) ===
.github/workflows/terraform.yml:86:      - name: install trivy
.github/workflows/terraform.yml:87:        uses: aquasecurity/setup-trivy@81e514348e19b6112ce2a7e3ecbafe19c1e1f567 # v0.3.1
.github/workflows/terraform.yml:90:          # v0.71.1 once failed to install via setup-trivy's get.trivy.dev path, so
.github/workflows/terraform.yml:92:          # renovate: datasource=github-releases depName=aquasecurity/trivy
.github/workflows/terraform.yml:96:      - name: trivy config scan (report + gate)
.github/workflows/terraform.yml:101:          #   - gate:   HIGH,CRITICAL, fails the job (exit-code 1) if any are found.
.github/workflows/terraform.yml:102:          trivy config . --format json --output trivy.json --exit-code 0
.github/workflows/terraform.yml:104:          echo "::group::Trivy findings (MEDIUM,HIGH,CRITICAL - report only)"
.github/workflows/terraform.yml:105:          trivy convert --format table --severity MEDIUM,HIGH,CRITICAL --exit-code 0 trivy.json
.github/workflows/terraform.yml:108:          trivy convert --format table --severity HIGH,CRITICAL --exit-code 1 trivy.json

aiTitle: Corriger les erreurs de lint dans la CI Terraform

content.text:
Le job progresse maintenant jusqu'au **trivy config scan** qui gate sur 1 HIGH (`AWS-0132`): le bucket S3, avec les valeurs par défaut du root (`enable_encryption=false`, `kms_key_arn=null`), n'a pas de chiffrement CMK. Le job échouait avant au tflint, donc trivy n'avait jamais tourné - ce n'est pas une régression de mes edits.

État CI après push: **commitlint ✓, fmt ✓, deferred-actions/02 ✓ (503 transitoire résolu), terraform-module-testing ✗ (trivy)**.

C'est un vrai conflit de design, pas du lint: le module rend le chiffrement **conditionnel exprès** et le test `no_encryption_block_without_key` asserte 0 bloc par défaut. Forcer le chiffrement casserait ce test. Le repo n'a aucune convention d'ignore trivy (les autres exemples sont secure-by-default). Prior art = l'exemple `deferred-actions` crée sa propre clé KMS au niveau root. Je te laisse trancher car ça touche le design pédagogique du live.

questions.question: Le gate trivy AWS-0132 (HIGH) exige un chiffrement CMK, mais le module rend le SSE-KMS conditionnel exprès (testé par assertion présence/absence). Comment résoudre ?

options.label: Root fournit sa clé KMS
options.description: Le root crée un aws_kms_key et l'injecte (enable_encryption=true, kms_key_arn=<clé>), comme l'exemple deferred-actions. Bucket chiffré par défaut, secure-by-default, trivy passe. Module + 4 fichiers de tests INCHANGÉS (la démo ressource conditionnelle reste intacte). Coût: +1 clé KMS créée à chaque apply du root. Mirror du live précédent = continuité de série.

options.label: Module chiffré par défaut
options.description: Encryption block toujours actif dans le module, CMK optionnelle avec fallback clé gérée aws/s3 (sse_algorithm=aws:kms, key id nullable) - exact pattern du module deferred-actions. Secure-by-default au niveau module. MAIS réécrit le bloc encryption + 2 run de plan.tftest.hcl (assertion présence/absence -> assertion type de clé), ce qui affaiblit la démo 'ressource conditionnelle count-based'.

options.label: Ignore trivy documenté
options.description: Ajoute #trivy:ignore:AWS-0132 commenté sur la ressource bucket, justifié (S3 chiffre déjà en SSE-S3/AES256 par défaut depuis 2023; CMK optionnelle = choix pédagogique volontaire). Minimal, tous les tests passent tels quels, zéro changement de design. MAIS introduit une convention d'ignore absente du repo et masque un signal sécurité sur un repo 'bonnes pratiques' public.

content.text: Tu as choisi le chiffrement par défaut au niveau module. Note importante: trivy évalue le module **via le root** (valeurs par défaut), donc je dois vérifier empiriquement si un `sse_algorithm=aws:kms` avec key id null suffit à AWS-0132, ou s'il faut une vraie CMK. Je reproduis le gate trivy en local pour itérer sans dépendre de la CI.

input.command: cd /Users/bgauduch/git/terraform-examples/examples/terraform-module-testing && which trivy && trivy --version 2>&1 | head -2 && echo "=== reproduce gate ===" && trivy config . --severity HIGH,CRITICAL --exit-code 1 --quiet 2>&1 | grep -iE "AWS-0|HIGH|CRITICAL|Failures|main.tf:|sse|kms|encrypt" | head -25; echo "exit: $?"

AWS-0132 (HIGH): Bucket does not encrypt data with a customer managed key.
Encryption using AWS keys provides protection for your S3 buckets. To gain greater control over encryption, such as key rotation, access policies, and auditability, use customer managed keys (CMKs) with SSE-KMS.

content.text:
Trivy AWS-0132 reproduit en local (v0.71.2). Le check exige une **CMK** (customer-managed key), pas juste `aws:kms`. Ça a une implication: ton choix "module chiffré par défaut" avec fallback clé aws/s3 ne suffirait pas à passer le gate (trivy évalue le root par défaut et veut une vraie CMK). Je dois cadrer le design précisément avant de coder.

Je suis passé en plan mode. Je lance l'exploration pour verrouiller les points pédagogiques à préserver et la sémantique exacte d'AWS-0132.

input.description: Inventory test teaching points
input.prompt:
Read-only exploration of a Terraform `terraform test` example. Do NOT edit anything.

Scope: /Users/bgauduch/git/terraform-examples/examples/terraform-module-testing/

Read ALL of these files fully:
- tests/validations.tftest.hcl
- tests/plan.tftest.hcl
- tests/deploy.tftest.hcl
- tests/parallel.tftest.hcl
- tests/setup/main.tf
- modules/s3-bucket/main.tf
- modules/s3-bucket/variables.tf
- modules/s3-bucket/outputs.tf
- modules/s3-bucket/versions.tf
- main.tf, variables.tf, outputs.tf, providers.tf
- any sweep.sh / scripts / README in the example

Produce a structured report:

1. TEST RUN INVENTORY: for each `run "..."` block across all .tftest.hcl files, list: file, run name, command (plan/apply), the module source it targets, the variables it sets, and EVERY assert condition (quote them verbatim). 

2. ENCRYPTION-COUPLED ASSERTIONS: explicitly flag which run blocks / assertions depend on the resource `aws_s3_bucket_server_side_encryption_configuration` being CONDITIONAL (present vs absent based on whether a KMS key is provided). Quote those assertions verbatim and give file:line. These are the ones that would BREAK if encryption becomes always-on.

3. CROSS-VARIABLE VALIDATIONS: list every `validation {}` block in modules/s3-bucket/variables.tf that references more than one variable (cross-variable). For each, quote the condition + error_message, and identify which test run exercises it via `expect_failures` (quote the expect_failures line + file:line). This tells us which teaching points about cross-variable validation must be preserved.

4. The `enable_encryption` variable: every place it is read/referenced across the whole example (module code, root, tests). Quote each occurrence with file:line.

5. The run-tagging / sweeper mechanism (run_id, default_tags, state_key, parallel) - summarize how it works and which files implement it.

Be exhaustive and quote exact code. Return findings only, no recommendations.

input.description: Trivy AWS-0132 + deferred-actions pattern
input.prompt:
Read-only investigation. Do NOT edit any files. You MAY run `trivy` (it is installed, v0.71.2) for read-only scanning.

GOAL 1 - Understand the repo's established secure S3 encryption pattern.
Scope: /Users/bgauduch/git/terraform-examples/examples/terraform-deferred-actions/
Read fully:
- 02-after/main.tf, 02-after/outputs.tf, 02-after/variables.tf
- 02-after/modules/s3-bucket-encrypted/main.tf, variables.tf, outputs.tf, versions.tf
- 01-before/main.tf and its module if different
Report the EXACT encryption pattern, quoting code with file:line:
- Is `aws_s3_bucket_server_side_encryption_configuration` always created (no count) or conditional?
- What is `sse_algorithm` set to? What is `kms_master_key_id` set to, and can it ever resolve to null?
- Does the ROOT module create its own KMS key (`aws_kms_key`) and pass its ARN to the child module? Quote how.
- Does the module ever rely on the aws/s3 default managed key, or always a customer-managed key (CMK)?

GOAL 2 - Determine EXACTLY what satisfies trivy check AWS-0132 ("Bucket does not encrypt data with a customer managed key").
- Run `trivy config <path> --severity HIGH,CRITICAL` on the deferred-actions/02-after example and confirm it PASSES (no AWS-0132). Report the command + result.
- Based on the deferred-actions code: does AWS-0132 require `kms_master_key_id` to be a NON-NULL customer key ARN, or does `sse_algorithm = "aws:kms"` alone (with null/absent key id) satisfy it? Find any evidence in the repo (any bucket that uses aws:kms WITHOUT a customer key and still passes trivy). 
- If still ambiguous, WebFetch https://avd.aquasec.com/misconfig/aws-0132 and report the exact trigger condition (when does it flag vs pass). State the conclusion clearly: "AWS-0132 passes IFF ...".

GOAL 3 - Enumerate ALL trivy findings on the target example so we avoid whack-a-mole.
- Run `trivy config /Users/bgauduch/git/terraform-examples/examples/terraform-module-testing --severity MEDIUM,HIGH,CRITICAL` and list EVERY finding (id, severity, resource, file:line, one-line title). We need to know if fixing AWS-0132 (HIGH, gated) will reveal other HIGH/CRITICAL findings that also gate, or other MEDIUM findings (reported but not gated).

Return findings only, with exact quotes and command outputs. No recommendations.

aiTitle: Corriger les erreurs de lint dans la CI Terraform

content.text:
Perfect. Now I have all the information needed. Let me compile the comprehensive report:

## TERRAFORM TEST EXAMPLE - COMPREHENSIVE REPORT

### 1. TEST RUN INVENTORY

#### `tests/validations.tftest.hcl`
- **Run name:** `bucket_name_too_short`
  - Command: `plan`
  - Module source: `./modules/s3-bucket`
  - Variables: `bucket_name = "ab"`, `environment = "dev"`
  - Assertions: None
  - Expect failures: `[var.bucket_name]`

- **Run name:** `bucket_name_invalid_chars`
  - Command: `plan`
  - Module source: `./modules/s3-bucket`
  - Variables: `bucket_name = "Mon_Bucket"`, `environment = "dev"`
  - Assertions: None
  - Expect failures: `[var.bucket_name]`

- **Run name:** `invalid_environment`
  - Command: `plan`
  - Module source: `./modules/s3-bucket`
  - Variables: `bucket_name = "valid-bucket-name"`, `environment = "production"`
  - Assertions: None
  - Expect failures: `[var.environment]`

- **Run name:** `encryption_requires_kms_key`
  - Command: `plan`
  - Module source: `./modules/s3-bucket`
  - Variables: `bucket_name = "valid-bucket-name"`, `environment = "dev"`, `enable_encryption = true`, `kms_key_arn = null`
  - Assertions: None
  - Expect failures: `[var.enable_encryption]`

- **Run name:** `prod_forbids_force_destroy`
  - Command: `plan`
  - Module source: `./modules/s3-bucket`
  - Variables: `bucket_name = "valid-bucket-name"`, `environment = "prod"`, `force_destroy = true`
  - Assertions: None
  - Expect failures: `[var.force_destroy]`

#### `tests/plan.tftest.hcl`
File-level variables block (applies to all runs):
```
variables {
  bucket_name = "demo-plan-bucket"
  environment = "dev"
}
```

- **Run name:** `versioning_enabled_by_default`
  - Command: `plan`
  - Module source: `./modules/s3-bucket`
  - Variables: (file defaults applied)
  - Assertions:
    - `condition     = aws_s3_bucket_versioning.this.versioning_configuration[0].status == "Enabled"`
    - `error_message = "versioning should default to Enabled"`

- **Run name:** `public_access_fully_blocked`
  - Command: `plan`
  - Module source: `./modules/s3-bucket`
  - Variables: (file defaults applied)
  - Assertions:
    - `condition = (aws_s3_bucket_public_access_block.this.block_public_acls && aws_s3_bucket_public_access_block.this.block_public_policy && aws_s3_bucket_public_access_block.this.ignore_public_acls && aws_s3_bucket_public_access_block.this.restrict_public_buckets)`
    - `error_message = "all four public access block flags must be true"`

- **Run name:** `no_encryption_block_without_key`
  - Command: `plan`
  - Module source: `./modules/s3-bucket`
  - Variables: `enable_encryption = false`, `kms_key_arn = null`
  - Assertions:
    - `condition     = length(aws_s3_bucket_server_side_encryption_configuration.this) == 0`
    - `error_message = "encryption config must be absent when no KMS key is provided"`

- **Run name:** `encryption_block_present_with_key`
  - Command: `plan`
  - Module source: `./modules/s3-bucket`
  - Variables: `enable_encryption = true`, `kms_key_arn = "arn:aws:kms:eu-west-1:111122223333:key/demo-key"`
  - Assertions:
    - `condition     = length(aws_s3_bucket_server_side_encryption_configuration.this) == 1`
    - `error_message = "encryption config must be present when a KMS key is provided"`
    - `condition     = one(aws_s3_bucket_server_side_encryption_configuration.this[0].rule).apply_server_side_encryption_by_default[0].kms_master_key_id == "arn:aws:kms:eu-west-1:111122223333:key/demo-key"`
    - `error_message = "the provided KMS key ARN must propagate to the encryption rule"`

#### `tests/deploy.tftest.hcl`
- **Run name:** `setup`
  - Command: (implicit apply)
  - Module source: `./tests/setup`
  - Variables: None specified
  - Assertions: None

- **Run name:** `deploy_and_assert`
  - Command: `apply`
  - Module source: (root, implicitly)
  - Variables: `bucket_name = "bga-tftest-${run.setup.suffix}"`, `environment = "dev"`, `versioning_enabled = true`, `force_destroy = true`
  - Assertions:
    - `condition     = output.bucket_id == "bga-tftest-${run.setup.suffix}"`
    - `error_message = "deployed bucket id must match the requested name"`
    - `condition     = output.versioning_status == "Enabled"`
    - `error_message = "versioning must be Enabled on the deployed bucket"`
    - `condition     = output.encryption_enabled == false`
    - `error_message = "encryption must be off when no KMS key is provided"`

#### `tests/parallel.tftest.hcl`
File-level variables block (applies to all runs):
```
variables {
  environment   = "dev"
  force_destroy = true
}
```

- **Run name:** `setup`
  - Command: (implicit apply)
  - Module source: `./tests/setup`
  - Variables: None specified
  - Assertions: None

- **Run name:** `deploy_a`
  - Command: `apply`
  - Module source: (root, implicitly)
  - State key: `a`
  - Parallel: `true`
  - Variables: `bucket_name = "bga-tftest-a-${run.setup.suffix}"`
  - Assertions:
    - `condition     = output.bucket_id == "bga-tftest-a-${run.setup.suffix}"`
    - `error_message = "bucket A name mismatch"`

- **Run name:** `deploy_b`
  - Command: `apply`
  - Module source: (root, implicitly)
  - State key: `b`
  - Parallel: `true`
  - Variables: `bucket_name = "bga-tftest-b-${run.setup.suffix}"`
  - Assertions:
    - `condition     = output.bucket_id == "bga-tftest-b-${run.setup.suffix}"`
    - `error_message = "bucket B name mismatch"`

---

### 2. ENCRYPTION-COUPLED ASSERTIONS (Conditional on `aws_s3_bucket_server_side_encryption_configuration`)

These assertions would break if encryption becomes always-on instead of conditional:

**File: `tests/plan.tftest.hcl`, lines 39-51** — Run `no_encryption_block_without_key`:
```hcl
assert {
  condition     = length(aws_s3_bucket_server_side_encryption_configuration.this) == 0
  error_message = "encryption config must be absent when no KMS key is provided"
}
```
This explicitly validates that the resource is ABSENT when encryption is disabled.

**File: `tests/plan.tftest.hcl`, lines 54-71** — Run `encryption_block_present_with_key`:
```hcl
assert {
  condition     = length(aws_s3_bucket_server_side_encryption_configuration.this) == 1
  error_message = "encryption config must be present when a KMS key is provided"
}
assert {
  condition     = one(aws_s3_bucket_server_side_encryption_configuration.this[0].rule).apply_server_side_encryption_by_default[0].kms_master_key_id == "arn:aws:kms:eu-west-1:111122223333:key/demo-key"
  error_message = "the provided KMS key ARN must propagate to the encryption rule"
}
```
These validate that the resource is PRESENT and contains the correct KMS key only when both `enable_encryption = true` AND `kms_key_arn` is provided.

**File: `tests/deploy.tftest.hcl`, lines 32-35** — Run `deploy_and_assert`:
```hcl
assert {
  condition     = output.encryption_enabled == false
  error_message = "encryption must be off when no KMS key is provided"
}
```
This asserts the computed output (derived from `local.kms_enabled` in the module) is `false`. Teaching point: outputs expose the conditional state for external validation.

---

### 3. CROSS-VARIABLE VALIDATIONS

#### Validation 1: Encryption requires KMS key
**File: `modules/s3-bucket/variables.tf`, lines 31-35**
```hcl
validation {
  condition     = !var.enable_encryption || var.kms_key_arn != null
  error_message = "kms_key_arn is required when enable_encryption is true."
}
```
**References:** `var.enable_encryption`, `var.kms_key_arn`
**Tested via:** `tests/validations.tftest.hcl` line 55, run `encryption_requires_kms_key`:
```hcl
expect_failures = [var.enable_encryption]
```
With variables: `enable_encryption = true`, `kms_key_arn = null` (line 52-53)

#### Validation 2: Prod forbids force_destroy
**File: `modules/s3-bucket/variables.tf`, lines 49-53**
```hcl
validation {
  condition     = !(var.environment == "prod" && var.force_destroy)
  error_message = "force_destroy must be false when environment is prod."
}
```
**References:** `var.environment`, `var.force_destroy`
**Tested via:** `tests/validations.tftest.hcl` line 68, run `prod_forbids_force_destroy`:
```hcl
expect_failures = [var.force_destroy]
```
With variables: `environment = "prod"`, `force_destroy = true` (lines 65-66)

---

### 4. THE `enable_encryption` VARIABLE: ALL REFERENCES

#### Module source (controls conditional logic):
**File: `modules/s3-bucket/main.tf`, line 4**
```hcl

```

#### Module variable definition and cross-validation:
**File: `modules/s3-bucket/variables.tf`, lines 26-36**
```hcl
variable "enable_encryption" {
  description = "Enable SSE-KMS encryption with a customer-managed key."

  validation {

    error_message = "kms_key_arn is required when enable_encryption is true."

```


**File: `tests/validations.tftest.hcl`**

- Line 55: `expect_failures = [var.enable_encryption]`

### 5. RUN-TAGGING / SWEEPER MECHANISM (run_id, default_tags, state_key, parallel)

#### **default_tags mechanism (AWS provider):**
**File: `providers.tf`, lines 17-22**
```hcl
default_tags {
  tags = {
    "tftest-suite" = "terraform-module-testing"
    "tftest-run"   = var.run_id
  }
}
```
Every resource deployed via this provider automatically carries two tags:
- `tftest-suite` = suite identifier (static, suite-wide, used by sweeper)
- `tftest-run` = unique run identifier (dynamic, per-test-run, for granular tracking)

#### **run_id variable (root):**
**File: `variables.tf`, lines 42-46**
```hcl
variable "run_id" {
  description = "Unique identifier for a test run, used for sweeper tagging."
  type        = string
  default     = "local"
}
```
Default is `"local"` for manual runs; CI or test orchestration can override with a unique identifier per invocation.

#### **state_key mechanism (isolation for parallel runs):**
**File: `tests/parallel.tftest.hcl`, lines 17-28 and 30-41**
```hcl
run "deploy_a" {
  command   = apply
  parallel  = true
  state_key = "a"
  ...
}

run "deploy_b" {
  command   = apply
  parallel  = true
  state_key = "b"
  ...
}
```
- `state_key` (Terraform 1.11+): distinct state files per run, preventing collision
- `parallel = true` (Terraform 1.12+): allows concurrent execution of these runs

#### **Sweeper script (cleanup):**
**File: `sweep.sh`, lines 14-23**
```bash
TAG_KEY="tftest-suite"
TAG_VALUE="terraform-module-testing"
REGION="${AWS_REGION:-eu-west-1}"

mapfile -t ARNS < <(aws resourcegroupstaggingapi get-resources \
  --region "$REGION" \
  --tag-filters "Key=${TAG_KEY},Values=${TAG_VALUE}" \
  --resource-type-filters "s3" \
  --query 'ResourceTagMappingList[].ResourceARN' --output text | tr '\t' '\n')
```
The sweeper queries AWS Resource Groups Tagging API for all S3 buckets carrying the `tftest-suite=terraform-module-testing` tag. The tag comes from `default_tags` in `providers.tf`. Command-line modes:
- `./sweep.sh` — dry-run (list buckets)
- `./sweep.sh --force` — empty and delete buckets

**How it works end-to-end:**
1. Every `terraform test` run applies resources tagged with `default_tags` from `providers.tf`
2. If the test completes normally, `terraform test` auto-destroys all resources
3. If interrupted, resources leak with tags intact
4. The sweeper scans for resources matching the suite tag (`tftest-suite=terraform-module-testing`)
5. Optional `tftest-run` tag enables future per-run cleanup tracking (not actively used in current sweeper, but variable is defined for that purpose)
6. `state_key` in parallel runs ensures each run has isolated state, preventing Terraform conflicts during concurrent apply/destroy

Perfect! Now I have all the information I need. Let me create a comprehensive summary of my findings:

## FINDINGS REPORT

### GOAL 1: Secure S3 Encryption Pattern Analysis

**Encryption Configuration Structure:**

The pattern uses `aws_s3_bucket_server_side_encryption_configuration` with conditional creation based on KMS key availability.

#### 02-after (Terraform 1.16+) Pattern:

**File: `/Users/bgauduch/git/terraform-examples/examples/terraform-deferred-actions/02-after/modules/s3-bucket-encrypted/main.tf:36-50`**

```terraform
resource "aws_s3_bucket_server_side_encryption_configuration" "this" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.this.id

  rule {
    apply_server_side_encryption_by_default {
      sse_algorithm = "aws:kms"
      kms_master_key_id = one(data.aws_kms_key.provided[*].arn)
      bucket_key_enabled = true
    }
  }
}
```

- **Is encryption configuration always created?** NO - it's created unconditionally here (no count), but the KMS key validation is conditional:

**File: `/Users/bgauduch/git/terraform-examples/examples/terraform-deferred-actions/02-after/modules/s3-bucket-encrypted/main.tf:1-12`**

```terraform
locals {
  use_provided_key = var.kms_key_arn != null
}

data "aws_kms_key" "provided" {
  count  = local.use_provided_key ? 1 : 0
  key_id = var.kms_key_arn
}
```

- **What is `sse_algorithm` set to?** `"aws:kms"` (line 41)
- **What is `kms_master_key_id` set to?** `one(data.aws_kms_key.provided[*].arn)` (line 44) - **This resolves to null when no key is provided** (empty array → null)
- **Does the ROOT module create its own KMS key?** YES:

**File: `/Users/bgauduch/git/terraform-examples/examples/terraform-deferred-actions/02-after/main.tf:11-17`**

```terraform
resource "aws_kms_key" "root" {
  description = "Root-managed KMS key for the deferred-actions S3 demo"
  enable_key_rotation = true
  deletion_window_in_days = 7
}
```

**Passing it to the module (line 23):**
```terraform
module "bucket" {
  source = "./modules/s3-bucket-encrypted"
  bucket_name = "${var.bucket_prefix}-${random_id.suffix.hex}"
  kms_key_arn = aws_kms_key.root.arn
}
```

- **Does it ever rely on aws/s3 default managed key?** YES - When `kms_key_arn` is null, the module falls back to S3's default managed key (aws/s3), per the comment in line 2-3 of the module.

#### 01-before (Pre-1.16) Pattern - Functionally identical:

**File: `/Users/bgauduch/git/terraform-examples/examples/terraform-deferred-actions/01-before/modules/s3-bucket-encrypted/main.tf:36-47`**

Same encryption configuration, same conditional data source, but with different input structure:
- Uses `var.kms.provided` (boolean, plan-known) and `var.kms.arn` (value, may be unknown)
- Still supports null/fallback to aws/s3

---

### GOAL 2: AWS-0132 Check Requirements

**Command run:**
```bash
trivy config /Users/bgauduch/git/terraform-examples/examples/terraform-deferred-actions/02-after --severity HIGH,CRITICAL
```

**Result:** PASSES - No findings reported
```
Report Summary
│ .                                   │ terraform │         0         │
│ modules/s3-bucket-encrypted/main.tf │ terraform │         0         │
```

**Conclusion on AWS-0132 requirement:**

AWS-0132 requires **`kms_master_key_id` to be a NON-NULL customer key ARN**. Evidence:

1. The `terraform-deferred-actions/02-after` example passes trivy despite having `kms_master_key_id = one(data.aws_kms_key.provided[*].arn)`, which **resolves to null when no key is provided** (because the encryption config is ALWAYS created, but uses a nullable expression for the key id).

2. The `terraform-module-testing/modules/s3-bucket` example FAILS AWS-0132 because:
   - **File: `/Users/bgauduch/git/terraform-examples/examples/terraform-module-testing/modules/s3-bucket/main.tf:29-41`**
   ```terraform
   resource "aws_s3_bucket_server_side_encryption_configuration" "this" {
     count = local.kms_enabled ? 1 : 0
     bucket = aws_s3_bucket.this.id
     rule {
       apply_server_side_encryption_by_default {
         sse_algorithm     = "aws:kms"
         kms_master_key_id = var.kms_key_arn
       }
       bucket_key_enabled = true
     }
   }
   ```
   
   The bucket resource (line 7-11) is evaluated **without the encryption config** being unconditionally attached. When `count = 0` (encryption disabled), trivy sees a bucket with no encryption configuration at all, triggering AWS-0132.

**AWS-0132 passes IFF:** The bucket resource has an attached `aws_s3_bucket_server_side_encryption_configuration` with `sse_algorithm = "aws:kms"` AND `kms_master_key_id` is populated with a **non-null customer-managed key ARN** (not the aws/s3 default).

---

Perfect! Now I have all the information I need. Let me create a comprehensive summary of my findings:

## FINDINGS REPORT

### GOAL 1: Secure S3 Encryption Pattern Analysis

**Encryption Configuration Structure:**

The pattern uses `aws_s3_bucket_server_side_encryption_configuration` with conditional creation based on KMS key availability.

#### 02-after (Terraform 1.16+) Pattern:

**File: `/Users/bgauduch/git/terraform-examples/examples/terraform-deferred-actions/02-after/modules/s3-bucket-encrypted/main.tf:36-50`**

```terraform
resource "aws_s3_bucket_server_side_encryption_configuration" "this" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.this.id

  rule {
    apply_server_side_encryption_by_default {
      sse_algorithm = "aws:kms"
      kms_master_key_id = one(data.aws_kms_key.provided[*].arn)
      bucket_key_enabled = true
    }
  }
}
```

- **Is encryption configuration always created?** NO - it's created unconditionally here (no count), but the KMS key validation is conditional:

**File: `/Users/bgauduch/git/terraform-examples/examples/terraform-deferred-actions/02-after/modules/s3-bucket-encrypted/main.tf:1-12`**

```terraform
locals {
  use_provided_key = var.kms_key_arn != null
}

data "aws_kms_key" "provided" {
  count  = local.use_provided_key ? 1 : 0
  key_id = var.kms_key_arn
}
```

- **What is `sse_algorithm` set to?** `"aws:kms"` (line 41)
- **What is `kms_master_key_id` set to?** `one(data.aws_kms_key.provided[*].arn)` (line 44) - **This resolves to null when no key is provided** (empty array → null)
- **Does the ROOT module create its own KMS key?** YES:

**File: `/Users/bgauduch/git/terraform-examples/examples/terraform-deferred-actions/02-after/main.tf:11-17`**

```terraform
resource "aws_kms_key" "root" {
  description = "Root-managed KMS key for the deferred-actions S3 demo"
  enable_key_rotation = true
  deletion_window_in_days = 7
}
```

**Passing it to the module (line 23):**
```terraform
module "bucket" {
  source = "./modules/s3-bucket-encrypted"
  bucket_name = "${var.bucket_prefix}-${random_id.suffix.hex}"
  kms_key_arn = aws_kms_key.root.arn
}
```

- **Does it ever rely on aws/s3 default managed key?** YES - When `kms_key_arn` is null, the module falls back to S3's default managed key (aws/s3), per the comment in line 2-3 of the module.

#### 01-before (Pre-1.16) Pattern - Functionally identical:

**File: `/Users/bgauduch/git/terraform-examples/examples/terraform-deferred-actions/01-before/modules/s3-bucket-encrypted/main.tf:36-47`**

Same encryption configuration, same conditional data source, but with different input structure:
- Uses `var.kms.provided` (boolean, plan-known) and `var.kms.arn` (value, may be unknown)
- Still supports null/fallback to aws/s3

Les deux agents convergent, avec une clarification cruciale sur AWS-0132:

**AWS-0132 passe SSI** le bucket a un `aws_s3_bucket_server_side_encryption_configuration` toujours attaché, `sse_algorithm="aws:kms"`, ET `kms_master_key_id` résout vers une **CMK non-null** dans l'éval statique du root. (deferred-actions passe parce que son root crée `aws_kms_key.root` et l'injecte toujours.)

Conséquence pour ton choix B: le fallback "clé gérée aws/s3" que j'avais décrit **ne passe pas** le gate. Pour que le **module** soit chiffré par défaut (sans dépendre du root), il doit **créer sa propre CMK** quand aucune n'est fournie. Ça rend le flag `enable_encryption` (on/off) incohérent puisque c'est toujours chiffré.

Bonne nouvelle: 1 seule finding trivy (AWS-0132), pas de whack-a-mole.

Je lis les derniers fichiers critiques avant de cadrer le design.

file.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Sweeper: cleans up orphaned test buckets (left by a `terraform test` interrupted
# before its auto-destroy). Targets resources carrying the suite tag.
# Tag source of truth: providers.tf (default_tags tftest-suite).
#
# Usage:
#   ./sweep.sh           # list tagged buckets (dry-run)
#   ./sweep.sh --force   # empty then delete each tagged bucket
#
# Requires AWS_PROFILE (e.g. sandbox). Tooled alternative: aws-nuke / awsweeper
# filtered on the same tag.
set -euo pipefail

TAG_KEY="tftest-suite"
TAG_VALUE="terraform-module-testing"
REGION="${AWS_REGION:-eu-west-1}"
FORCE="${1:-}"

mapfile -t ARNS < <(aws resourcegroupstaggingapi get-resources \
  --region "$REGION" \
  --tag-filters "Key=${TAG_KEY},Values=${TAG_VALUE}" \
  --resource-type-filters "s3" \
  --query 'ResourceTagMappingList[].ResourceARN' --output text | tr '\t' '\n')

if [ "${#ARNS[@]}" -eq 0 ] || [ -z "${ARNS[0]:-}" ]; then
  echo "No bucket tagged ${TAG_KEY}=${TAG_VALUE}."
  exit 0
fi

echo "Tagged buckets found:"
printf '  %s\n' "${ARNS[@]}"

if [ "$FORCE" != "--force" ]; then
  echo "Dry-run. Re-run with --force to empty + delete."
  exit 0
fi

for arn in "${ARNS[@]}"; do
  bucket="${arn##*:::}"
  echo "Deleting ${bucket} ..."
  aws s3 rm "s3://${bucket}" --recursive >/dev/null 2>&1 || true
  aws s3api delete-bucket --bucket "${bucket}" --region "$REGION"
done
echo "Cleanup done."

content.text:
Design complet en main. Le choix B impose que le module **crée sa propre CMK** quand aucune n'est fournie (seul moyen de passer AWS-0132 sans dépendre du root). Ça rend `enable_encryption` (on/off) incohérent et déplace la ressource conditionnelle vers la création de clé. Un dernier arbitrage pédagogique t'appartient avant que je fige le plan.

Note commune aux deux variantes: le sweeper (`sweep.sh`, aujourd'hui S3-only) devra aussi nettoyer les CMK taguées, sinon fuite de clés sur test interrompu.

questions.question: Le module chiffré par défaut doit créer sa propre CMK quand aucune n'est fournie -> le flag on/off `enable_encryption` perd son sens. Quelle variante pour la variable et la démo de validation croisée ?

options.label: Drop, gate sur kms_key_arn
options.description: Supprime enable_encryption (module + root). Logique: clé fournie -> utilisée (aws_kms_key.this count=0); sinon le module crée aws_kms_key.this (count=1). SSE-KMS toujours attaché, kms_master_key_id = coalesce(fournie, créée). La ressource conditionnelle (démo plan-assert) devient la clé créée. On PERD le run de validation croisée encryption_requires_kms_key, mais la démo expect_failures reste via prod_forbids_force_destroy. API simple, secure-by-default. Aligne avec 'simplicity first'.

options.label: Garder enable_encryption
options.description: enable_encryption (défaut true) repurposé = 'chiffrer avec une CMK': true -> clé fournie ou auto-créée; false -> clé gérée aws/s3 (runtime, trivy voit le défaut=true donc passe). Nouvelle validation croisée (kms_key_arn interdit si enable_encryption=false) pour garder la démo expect_failures + un run testant le chemin aws/s3. Plus fidèle, plus de surface de test, mais interplay 3 variables = plus complexe à maintenir.

content.content: Your questions have been answered: "Le module chiffré par défaut doit créer sa propre CMK quand aucune n'est fournie -> le flag on/off `enable_encryption` perd son sens. Quelle variante pour la variable et la démo de validation croisée ?"="Garder enable_encryption". You can now continue with these answers in mind.

SSE-KMS toujours attaché, kms_master_key_id = coalesce(fournie, créée). La ressource conditionnelle (démo plan-assert) devient la clé créée. On PERD le run de validation croisée encryption_requires_kms_key, mais la démo expect_failures reste via prod_forbids_force_destroy. API simple, secure-by-default. Aligne avec 'simplicity first'.

options.description: enable_encryption (défaut true) repurposé = 'chiffrer avec une CMK': true -> clé fournie ou auto-créée; false -> clé gérée aws/s3 (runtime, trivy voit le défaut=true donc passe). Nouvelle validation croisée (kms_key_arn interdit si enable_encryption=false) pour garder la démo expect_failures + un run testant le chemin aws/s3. # Plan - Fix CI on PR #33 (terraform-module-testing)

## Context

PR #33 (`feat/terraform-module-testing`, repo `bgauduch/terraform-examples`) adds the live's
native `terraform test` example. CI had 3 red checks. Two are **already fixed and pushed**
(commit `b384bf0`, force-pushed):
- **Conventional commits** - commit body line was 120 chars; reflowed to <=100. GREEN.
- **terraform fmt / deferred-actions/02-after** - the latter was a transient 503 on trivy
  attestation download; the re-push re-ran it. GREEN.
- tflint findings in the example (missing `required_version`/provider version in
  `tests/setup/main.tf`, unused `enable_encryption`) - fixed. tflint now exits 0.
  Version constraints later corrected to root-style `~>` per HashiCorp best practice
  (root modules: `~>` lower+upper bound; reusable modules: `>=`). User also translated
  touched-file comments FR->EN.

**Remaining red check:** `validate + lint + scan (terraform-module-testing)` now fails at the
**trivy** step (it previously failed earlier at tflint, so trivy never ran). Single finding:

> **AWS-0132 (HIGH)** - `modules/s3-bucket/main.tf:7-11` - Bucket does not encrypt data with a
> customer managed key.

Root cause: the module makes SSE-KMS **conditional** (`count = kms_enabled ? 1 : 0`); with the
root's default vars (`enable_encryption=false`, `kms_key_arn=null`) the encryption resource is
absent, so trivy (static scan of the root default path) sees an unencrypted-by-CMK bucket and
gates the job (HIGH,CRITICAL -> exit 1). Confirmed locally with trivy 0.71.2. Only this one
finding (no whack-a-mole).

**Verified fact (both Explore agents):** AWS-0132 passes IFF the bucket has an always-attached
`aws_s3_bucket_server_side_encryption_configuration` with `sse_algorithm="aws:kms"` AND
`kms_master_key_id` resolving to a **non-null CMK** in the root's default eval. The sister
`deferred-actions/02-after` example passes because its root creates `aws_kms_key.root` and
always injects it.

## Decision (user-chosen)

Make the **module encrypt by default with a CMK** (secure-by-default at the module level, not
patched at the root), and **keep `enable_encryption`** (repurposed). The module **creates its
own CMK** when none is provided - the only way to satisfy AWS-0132 without the root supplying a
key.

New semantics:
- `enable_encryption` (default **true**) = "encrypt with a customer-managed key (CMK)".
  - `true` + `kms_key_arn` set -> use the provided CMK.
  - `true` + `kms_key_arn` null -> **module creates a dedicated `aws_kms_key`** and uses it.
  - `false` -> SSE-KMS with the **aws/s3 default managed key** (runtime path; trivy sees the
    default `true`, so the gate passes).
- The **conditional resource** (for the plan-assertion teaching point) moves from the encryption
  block to the **KMS key creation** (`aws_kms_key.this`, created only when no key is provided).
- Cross-variable validation + `expect_failures` demo is **preserved** by inverting the rule:
  `kms_key_arn` is forbidden when `enable_encryption=false`.

Comments in every touched file go **EN** (matches the user's FR->EN migration of this repo).
